findings.description: fetch_events docstring (line 32) promises an inclusive [start, end] range, but the SQL uses created_at < end.isoformat(); since created_at holds timestamps and end is a date, every same-day event on the 'end' boundary (e.g. '2026-06-10 09:30:00' > '2026-06-10' lexicographically) is silently excluded, so the entire end day is dropped and counts are under-reported in violation of the stated contract.

The docstring 'both boundary days included' directly contradicts the half-open SQL predicate on line 38, so callers relying on the documented inclusive semantics will get incorrect (truncated) results regardless of which side is 'intended' — code and spec must be reconciled.

top_users (line 68) and event_count (lines 82, 88) repeat the same created_at < end boundary exclusion as fetch_events, so any caller passing the same date range to these methods expecting the end day to be counted gets a silently inconsistent / under-counted result across the module's reporting surface.

display_name and action are interpolated raw into HTML via f-strings (lines 50-52) and title via .format() (line 54) with no escaping, so user-controlled values containing markup produce stored/reflected HTML/script injection in the admin dashboard; flagged in passing per logic-only scope — defer exploit detail to critic-security.

save_report performs no validation or error handling around open()/write(), so a bad out_path, permission error, or full disk raises an unannotated exception to the caller (it propagates rather than being swallowed, so this is correctness-borderline, not a silent-failure bug).
